department: Civil and Environmental Engineering / Mechanical Engineering
lab_name: Laboratory for Atomistic and Molecular Mechanics (LAMM)
field_a: Computational materials science and molecular mechanics
active_in_field_a: Develops AI-driven multi-agent models for materials discovery. Uses molecular dynamics, machine learning, and atomistic simulation to design biological and synthetic materials from the molecular scale up. Recent work includes paraffin-enabled graphene transfer (Nature Communications), peptide design for cancer-relevant receptors, and lightweight ceramics. Lab has supercomputing, 3D printing, and hydrothermal reactor capabilities.
active_in_field_b: Composes classical, electronic, and experimental music. Developed a method to translate the 20 amino acid types into a 20-tone musical scale, then used AI to generate new protein sequences from modified melodies. Translated the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein into a musical score performed by South Korea's Lindenbaum Festival Orchestra. Member of MIT CAST (Center for Art, Science and Technology). Recent work on unsupervised cross-domain translation between music and protein design, and formal analogies between Bach chorales and phononic crystals.
cross_domain_note: Graduate students work on AI systems that simultaneously reason about protein folding mechanics AND musical structure, treating both as graph-representable sequence problems. The lab's ScienceClaw system generates results spanning cricket wings, phononic crystals, and Bach chorales in a single framework.
potential_crossing: The bidirectional music-to-protein pipeline could discover structural motifs in materials that are 'heard' but never 'seen' by conventional simulation, enabling intuition-driven materials design.
verification_notes: Buehler publishes actively in both domains as of 2025. SoundCloud page with compositions. ACS Nano paper on sonification method. Co-Founder and CTO at Unreasonable Labs.

department: Materials Science and Engineering / Brain and Cognitive Sciences
active_in_field_a: Designs and fabricates multifunctional, multimaterial fibers using thermal drawing processes. Synthesizes magnetoelectric nanodiscs (core-double-shell Fe3O4-CoFe2O4-BaTiO3 architecture, 250nm diameter). Develops magnetic nanomaterial transducers that convert magnetic fields into neural signals. Published in Nature Nanotechnology (Jan 2025) on magnetoelectric nanodiscs for wireless neuromodulation.
active_in_field_b: Probes how peripheral organ signaling in the gut shapes brain dynamics, behavior, and neurological disease. Uses fiber-based probes for simultaneous optical, electrical, and chemical interrogation of neural circuits in the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral organs. Investigating gut-brain communication circuits using the materials tools her lab builds.
cross_domain_note: Students must master both fiber drawing / nanoparticle synthesis AND in vivo neuroscience experiments in animal models. The lab explicitly operates 'at the interface of materials science, electronics, and neurobiology.' Named head of DMSE in 2024 while holding appointment in Brain and Cognitive Sciences.
potential_crossing: Wireless magnetic nanomaterials originally designed for brain stimulation could become a new modality for interrogating the enteric nervous system, potentially revealing materials-science-tractable mechanisms behind gut-brain disorders.
verification_notes: Named DMSE department head July 2024. Active publications in both Nature Nanotechnology (materials) and neuroscience journals as of 2025. Co-founded Neurobionics Inc. in 2023.

department: Chemical Engineering / Biology and Biological Engineering
field_b: Synthetic biology and microbial genetic engineering
active_in_field_a: Develops advanced ultrasound imaging methods including amplitude-modulated singular value decomposition (IEEE TMI, 2025) and deactivation super-resolution for single-cell acoustic localization (npj Acoustics, 2025). Engineers acoustic metamaterial concepts for focused ultrasound neuromodulation. Builds hardware and signal processing pipelines for non-invasive deep-tissue imaging.
active_in_field_b: Engineers gas vesicles -- hollow protein nanostructures from buoyant microbes -- as genetically encoded reporters. Designs synthetic gene circuits in bacteria and mammalian cells that express gas vesicles on demand, creating living ultrasound contrast agents. Develops genetically encoded MRI reporters and magnetic bioswitches. Builds cell-based therapeutics with acoustic readout.
cross_domain_note: The lab explicitly works at 'the interface of molecular and cellular engineering with various forms of physical energy: magnetic, mechanical, thermal and chemical.' Students routinely do both acoustic physics experiments AND molecular cloning / microbial engineering. Collaborators span INSERM Paris, Harvard, and multiple Caltech departments.
potential_crossing: Genetically encoded acoustic reporters could enable real-time ultrasound monitoring of engineered microbial therapeutics inside the body, merging synthetic biology's programmability with ultrasound's non-invasive depth penetration.
verification_notes: Multiple 2025 publications in both acoustic engineering (IEEE TMI, npj Acoustics) and synthetic biology. Lab page lists active projects in both domains. Vilcek Foundation Prize recipient.

field_b: Ultra-low-cost device engineering for global health
active_in_field_a: Studies fundamental physics of marine plankton behavior. In 2024, discovered that dinoflagellate Pyrocystis noctiluca inflates 6-fold post cell division in under 10 minutes, selectively importing low-density fluid to reverse sedimentation. Published on coldest recorded eukaryotic motility (-15C in Arctic). Developed PlanktoScope for citizen-science ocean biodiversity monitoring. Holds courtesy appointment in Stanford Oceans department. Collaborates with Schmidt Ocean Institute.
active_in_field_b: Invented the Foldscope (paper microscope, 2.5M+ distributed worldwide, now on 415th iteration), Paperfuge (20-cent centrifuge), LollipopScope, Octopi (automated malaria/TB diagnostic platform), OScan (smartphone oral cancer scanner), and Pufferfish (open-source ICU ventilator). Developed punch-card-programmable microfluidics ($5 chemistry kit). ESPressoscope published 2024.
cross_domain_note: Lab members include specialists in machine learning, fluid mechanics, cell biology, oceanography, optics, architecture, and visual arts. Individual researchers contribute to both ocean biophysics projects (e.g. sedimentation dynamics of marine snow) and engineering tool projects (e.g. Octopi diagnostics platform). The lab's 'curiosity-driven' ethos explicitly bridges both.
potential_crossing: Plankton fluid-dynamics insights could yield new passive microfluidic separation mechanisms for the frugal diagnostic devices, enabling gravity-driven sample prep in settings without centrifuges.
verification_notes: 2024 publications in both plankton biophysics (Stanford Report, BioRxiv) and device engineering (ESPressoscope). NIH WALS 2024-2025 lecture on frugal science. Active courtesy appointments in Biology and Oceans.

field_a: Advanced materials characterization (atom probe tomography, synchrotron methods)
field_b: Marine organism biology (sea urchin embryology, chiton biology, acantharian ecology)
active_in_field_a: Pioneered atom probe tomography (APT) for mineralized tissues. Developed synchrotron scanning Mossbauer spectroscopy, sub-micron X-ray diffraction mapping, and synchrotron micro-CT for biological specimens. Uses microfluidic droplet reactors to study calcium carbonate crystallization pathways. Applies convolutional neural networks for segmenting micro-CT data of rodent jaws.
active_in_field_b: Studies how sea urchin primary mesenchyme cells control single-crystal calcite spicule growth using recombinant signaling molecules (rVEGF). Investigates how Acantharia (marine protists) selectively sequester strontium to form celestine crystals at concentrations vastly exceeding equilibrium. Studies how chitons incorporate amorphous iron phosphate into their radula teeth, producing the hardest biomineral known. Also studies human dental enamel pathology (caries, amelogenesis imperfecta, fluorosis).
cross_domain_note: Students must be fluent in both high-vacuum materials characterization methods (atom probe, synchrotron beamlines) AND marine organism culture and embryology. The same student might operate a $2M atom probe on Monday and culture sea urchin embryos on Wednesday.
potential_crossing: Understanding how acantharians concentrate strontium 10,000x above seawater levels under ambient conditions could unlock room-temperature synthesis routes for piezoelectric strontium compounds, bypassing the high-temperature processing that dominates industrial ceramic manufacturing.
verification_notes: Active lab website with current projects listed. Recent publications on chiton teeth, sea urchin spicules, and enamel. NSF-funded research (Dr. Keith Alvares). Google Scholar shows continuous publication in both domains.

field_a: Ultrafast organismic biophysics (insect mechanics, worm locomotion, cellular motility)
field_b: Frugal science device engineering for global health
active_in_field_a: Studies extreme biomechanics at small scales: how sharpshooter insects exploit droplet superpulsion to excrete (featured in Nature), wax tail aerodynamics of planthopper nymphs, springtail jumping mechanics, 3D tracking of water-hopping mudskippers, adhesion in blackworm capture by freshwater planaria, and ultrafast light-controlled cells (Nature 2025). Publishes fundamental fluid dynamics and soft-matter physics papers.
active_in_field_b: Invented the 20-cent paper centrifuge (Paperfuge), the 23-cent electroporator, and the 96-cent hearing aid (LoCHAid). Runs the Frugal Science Academy at Georgia Tech. Tools designed for deployment in resource-limited healthcare settings worldwide. Schmidt Sciences grantee for frugal science.
cross_domain_note: The lab explicitly connects both tracks: insect urination physics directly informed better nozzle designs; springtail jump mechanics produced penny-sized jumping robots. Students are trained in both fundamental biophysics observation AND rapid prototyping of low-cost devices. The lab's identity is built around 'curiosity-driven science' spanning both.
potential_crossing: The springtail's latch-mediated spring mechanism, which achieves accelerations of 100g at millimeter scale, could be miniaturized into a mechanical trigger for point-of-care diagnostic devices that need precise single-use actuation without electronics.
verification_notes: Active publications in both tracks through 2025. Nature publication on ultrafast cells. Schmidt Sciences grant. Frugal Science Academy runs at Georgia Tech. NIH NIGMS Q&A profile (2023) describes dual-track research.

department: Computer Science / Agricultural and Biological Engineering (joint appointment)
field_a: Autonomous robotics, reinforcement learning, and multi-agent AI
field_b: Row-crop agriculture and under-canopy farm operations
active_in_field_a: Develops core autonomy algorithms: vision-based navigation using semantic keypoints (CropFollow++), multi-agent coordination, reinforcement learning for outdoor robots operating in harsh/uncertain environments, and robot perception systems. Publishes at RSS, ICRA, and other top robotics venues. Won RSS 2018 best systems paper.
active_in_field_b: Directs the USDA/NIFA Farm of the Future (I-FARM) site. Co-PI on AIFARMS National AI Institute. Builds robots (TerraSentia) that navigate under crop canopies to perform tasks infeasible for drones or large equipment: phenotyping, weed detection, targeted intervention. Studies socioeconomic barriers to AI adoption in farming. Joint appointment in Agricultural and Biological Engineering.
cross_domain_note: Students work on fundamental reinforcement learning and computer vision problems that are tested and deployed on actual farm fields. The lab's identity requires fluency in both ICRA-style robotics AND agronomic field trial methodology. Donald Biggar Willet Faculty Fellow.
potential_crossing: Under-canopy autonomous navigation algorithms, trained on the unique visual chaos of crop rows, could generalize to other 'cluttered natural corridor' domains like forest firefighting, cave exploration, or coral reef inspection where GPS-denied, visually dense environments defeat standard approaches.
verification_notes: Joint appointment in CS and Agricultural Engineering verified. USDA I-FARM directorship confirmed. TerraSentia robot actively deployed. CropFollow++ published. Illinois Robotics Group profile lists both domains.
